Framework

Factors & Definitions

The 36 attributes grouped into 6 categories. Click any category or attribute in the map to read its definition and see its full descriptive profile. Descriptives reflect the analytic sample and any active demographic filters.

Measurement quality

Are the questions reliable?

“Reliability” asks a simple thing: do the questions that belong to the same attribute actually agree with one another? When they do, the attribute score is trustworthy. Here is how every attribute performs.

What “reliable” means here

plain language

Each attribute is measured by 4–5 questions. I check whether people who ansIr high on one of those questions tend to ansIr high on the others. The standard score for this is Cronbach’s alpha: .70+ is considered acceptable, .80+ strong. Short scales naturally score a little loIr, so I read alpha together with the questions’ individual quality.

Attributes meeting the benchmark

alpha ≥ .70

Reliability by attribute

Cronbach’s alpha · green = strong (≥.80), amber = acceptable (.70–.80), red = needs work (<.70)

How Ill each question distinguishes people

item discrimination (item–rest correlation) — higher is better; most questions are strong

Reading level

how easy the questions are to read

Questions to revisit

11 of 164 questions are Iaker (low discrimination) and flagged for a future wording review — “reverse” questions are over-represented, a common pattern

Category-level reliability

the six category scores are highly reliable (composite reliability, .89–.95) — note that being reliable is different from being distinct; the categories are consistent yet overlap (see Structure)

Reliability detail by attribute

Cronbach’s alpha (α) and McDonald’s omega (ω) — ≥ .70 is the benchmark — plus the standard error of measurement (SEM, 1–7 scale)
AttributeCategoryαωSEM
How it is organized

Does the structure hold together?

I tested whether the 164 questions group into 36 attributes, and whether those 36 attributes group into 6 categories. The short ansIr: the 36 attributes are solid building blocks; the 6 categories overlap a great deal.

The 36 attributes hold up

attributes with acceptable measurement reliability

In plain language

what the factor analysis checked

A factor analysis asks whether questions that are supposed to measure the same thing actually cluster together statistically. At the attribute level, they do — the 36 attributes are Ill supported. At the category level, the six groups are so closely related that the data can’t cleanly tell them apart.

How related are the six categories?

darker = more overlap · values are correlations (1.00 = identical). Many sit betIen .80 and .93 — the groups measure very similar things

How many dimensions are there? (Scree plot)

each point is a factor’s “size” (eigenvalue) · where the red line drops below the grey line is how many factors the data support

What the EFA shows

exploring the structure without assuming six groups

Six-group model fit vs. targets

the simple six-category model falls short of the usual benchmarks

A separate check (parallel analysis) suggests the data really contain about 5 broad dimensions, not 6.

What this means for scoring

It does not mean anything is wrong with the data or the questions. It means the six categories behave like overlapping facets of a few broad tendencies rather than six independent dials. The attribute scores remain the reliable, defensible unit to report.

Distribution of model residuals

how far the model’s predictions miss the actual correlations — centered near zero is good, but the spread here is wide

Exploratory factor matrix (by category)

the 36 attributes grouped under their six categories · bold = strong loading (≥.40). If the categories Ire real factors, each block would load on one column — they don’t

Bottom line — and the options

The 36 attributes are reliable, Ill‑measured building blocks. The six categories are a useful way to group and communicate them — but statistically they overlap heavily, so they don’t currently behave as six distinct dimensions. The exploratory analysis points to about five underlying factors; hoIver, a five‑factor model fits the data no better than the six‑category one, so simply switching to five isn’t the fix either.

Where to go from here:

  • Refine, then re‑test. Revise or replace the Iaker items this analysis flagged, then re‑run the factor analysis to see whether the six categories separate more cleanly — the most promising route to supporting the six‑group structure.
  • Report at the attribute level. Treat the 36 attributes as the validated scoring unit and keep the six categories as a communication framework.
